1:
hallĕlûyāh — the psalm opens with the liturgical interjection that will frame the entire Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113–118). The term is a third-person plural imperative fused with the divine name: hallĕlû (praise, pl.) + yāh (the LORD). It stands outside the acrostic structure proper, which begins with ʾôdeh yhwh.

ʾôdeh yhwh bĕkhol-lēbāb — ʾôdeh is Hiphil of yādāh (to cast, throw), which came to mean "acknowledge" then "thank/praise." The phrase bĕkhol-lēbāb (with the whole heart) sets the register: total personal engagement, not rote liturgy. Ps 111 and 112 are twin acrostic poems — each of the 22 half-lines begins with successive Hebrew letters (aleph through taw).
2: gĕdōlîm maʿăśê yhwh — the acrostic continues: gimel opens this half-line. dĕrûšîm lĕkhol-ḥepṣêhem — dĕrûšîm is a passive participle of dāraš (to seek, study, inquire) — the works of the LORD are "being studied/sought out" by those who delight in them. This is not passive contemplation but active investigation; the same verb is used of studying Torah (Ezra 7:10).
3: hōd-wĕhādār pĕʿullātô — hōd (splendor, majesty) and hādār (honor, glory, adornment) are often paired for the royal appearance of kings; here applied to YHWH's deeds. wĕṣidqātô ʿōmedet lāʿad — his righteousness "stands forever" — the participle ʿōmedet conveys ongoing, present standing. This phrase recurs word-for-word in Psalms 112:3 and 112:9 applied to the righteous person — the mirroring is deliberate.
4: zēkher ʿāśāh lĕniplĕʾōtāyw — "he made a memorial/remembrance for his wonderful works." zēkher (memorial, remembrance) is the word behind commemorative ritual — Passover, the feasts, and later Eucharist all operate as zēkher. ḥannûn wĕraḥûm yhwh — the same paired attributes as Exodus 34:6, the foundational divine self-disclosure at Sinai. Ps 111 takes these attributes and distributes them through the psalm; Ps 112 applies them to the righteous person.
5: ṭeref nātan lîrēʾāyw — ṭeref literally means torn prey — what a wild animal has seized and torn. Used here for the provision God gives to those who fear him; the raw word choice suggests generous, abundant feeding, not measured rations. yizkōr lĕʿōlām bĕrîtô — "he will be mindful forever of his covenant" — zākar (remember) in the OT is never merely cognitive; divine remembering means divine action on behalf of the covenant.
6: kōaḥ maʿăśāyw higgîd lĕʿammô — "the power of his works he has declared to his people" — higgîd (Hiphil of nāgad, to make clear, declare) implies formal proclamation; the Exodus is the paradigm. lātēt lāhem naḥălat gôyim — to give them the nations' inheritance/possession. naḥălāh (inheritance, possession) carries the kinship/family estate meaning — the land given to Israel is a family grant from the divine father, not merely a military conquest.
7: maʿăśê yādāyw ʾĕmet ûmišpāṭ — "the works of his hands are faithfulness (ʾĕmet) and justice (mišpāṭ)" — the two together form a hendiadys for covenant order. ʾĕmet = truth/faithfulness (relational reliability); mišpāṭ = right judgment/justice (legal reliability). neʾĕmānîm kol-piqûdāyw — "all his precepts are trustworthy" — neʾĕmānîm = Niphal participle of ʾāman (to be firm, reliable) — the cognate of ʾāmēn.
8: sĕmûkhîm lāʿad lĕʿôlām — "upheld/supported for ever and ever" — sāmak means to lean on, support, prop up; the precepts of YHWH are themselves load-bearing structures. ʿăśûyim bĕʾĕmet wĕyāšār — "done/made in faithfulness and uprightness" (yāšār = straight, right, upright).
9: pĕdût šālaḥ lĕʿammô — "redemption (pĕdût) he sent to his people" — pĕdût is related to pādāh (to ransom, redeem by payment), distinct from gĕʾullāh (kinship redemption). The verb šālaḥ (send, commission) gives the redemption a missional character — it was dispatched. ṣiwwāh-lĕʿōlām bĕrîtô — "he commanded his covenant forever" — ṣiwwāh (command) used with the covenant is striking: the covenant is not merely offered but decreed. qādôš wĕnôrāʾ šĕmô — "holy and awesome/fearful is his name" — the psalm's climax before the concluding wisdom formula.
10: rēʾshît ḥokhmāh yirʾat yhwh — "the beginning/chief part of wisdom is the fear of the LORD." rēʾshît can mean either "beginning" (first step, entry point) or "the best/foremost part" (as in firstfruits, Genesis 1:1). Both meanings apply: fearing the LORD is where wisdom starts and also its highest expression. This clause appears nearly verbatim in Proverbs 9:10 and (slightly varied) in Proverbs 1:7, marking Ps 111 as part of the wisdom tradition. The acrostic ends with taw in the final word tĕhillātô (his praise): the whole alphabet praises him.
